title: Active Inference and Ecology
subtitle: A research scaffold for organisms, collectives, environments, and adaptive field systems.
audience: Ecologists, environmental modelers, collective-behavior researchers, and applied field teams.
lede: Ecological applications of Active Inference ask how organisms, groups, and instruments infer environmental states while acting within them.

sections.heading: Why the domain fits
sections.body: Ecology involves adaptive agents, uncertain environments, niche construction, and multi-scale feedback. Active Inference can organize those questions around perception, action, and viable states.

sections.heading: Application pattern
sections.body: A domain report should distinguish biological theory, behavioral ecology, collective foraging, geospatial inference, and applied sensing. These layers share concepts but require different evidence and validation standards.

sections.heading: Evidence to collect next
sections.body: The next pass should gather work on collective behavior, foraging, adaptive environmental monitoring, ecosystem modeling, and geospatial decision support, then classify each source by organism, scale, and intervention type.

sections.heading: Reference Backbone
sections.body: Karl J. Friston (2010). The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory? Nature Reviews Neuroscience. DOI: 10.1038/nrn2787. Christopher L. Buckley, Chang Sub Kim, Simon McGregor, Anil K. Seth (2017). The free energy principle for action and perception: A mathematical review. Journal of Mathematical Psychology. DOI: 10.1016/j.jmp.2017.09.004. Giovanni Pezzulo, Francesco Rigoli, Karl J. Friston (2017). Active Inference, homeostatic regulation and adaptive behavioural control. Progress in Neurobiology. DOI: 10.1016/j.pneurobio.2017.08.001. Maxwell J. D. Ramstead, Karl J. Friston, Axel Constant, Lancelot Da Costa, Casper Hesp, Beren Millidge, Alexander Tschantz (2023). On Bayesian Mechanics: A Physics of and by Beliefs. arXiv.

cards.title: Organisms and niches
cards.text: Study action and perception as a coupled fit between agent and environment.

cards.title: Collectives
cards.text: Track when the model describes individuals, groups, or ecosystem-level dynamics.

cards.title: Field systems
cards.text: Connect theory to sensing, geospatial data, and adaptive monitoring workflows.
